Oracc: CAMS/GKAB — CAMS Geography of Knowledge Corpus

STT 2, 238 [Instructions against bed wetting][via cams/gkab]

Obverse
o 1o 1

* NA ina KI.NU₂-šu₂ KAŠ₃ iš-tin!(SIG) ŠU dNIN.⸢MAḪ⸣1

(o 1) If a man has urinated in his bed: Hand of Ninmah.

o 22

DU₃.DU₃-BI gišNU₂ šu₂-a-⸢tu₂⸣ ta-za-qap₂!(RU) SIG₄ ⸢ša₂?⸣-ḫa ŠUBdi!(.AK)

(o 2) Its ritual: You set that bed upright (and) lay down a brick at right angles (to it);

o 33

KEŠ₂ KEŠ₂as udu⸢SISKUR⸣ BALqi₂ dugA.DA.⸢GUR₅⸣ GUBan KAŠ u GEŠTIN ⸢BAL⸣

(o 3) you set up a ritual arrangement, perform a sacrifice, set up an adagurru-libation vessel, (and) libate beer and wine;

o 44

GURUN KIRI₆ DU₃.A.⸢BI⸣ GARan NIG₂.NA šimLI ⸢ta⸣-sar-raq uš-[ken]2

(o 4) you provide all sorts of garden fruit (and) scatter juniper aromatics over a censer. Then he kneels down (and says):

o 55

EN₂ dNIN.⸢MAḪ DU₃?at?⸣ [AN] ⸢u KIti be⸣-[let i?]-⸢li? at⸣-[ti]

(o 5) Incantation: "You, Ninmah, creator of [heaven] and earth, mistress of the gods;

o 66

dNIN.MAḪ ⸢SILIM⸣[ma? šuk?]-ni dNIN.MAḪ ⸢nap⸣-šir a-⸢a⸣-[ši]3

(o 6) "Ninmah, bring about reconciliation; Ninmah, relent towards me;

o 77

dNIN.MAḪ [suḫ]-ḫi-ri pa-ni-ki 7šu₂ DU₁₁.GA!(GAR-)⸢ma ma-gir?⸣

(o 7) "Ninmah, turn your face!" He will say it seven times and then he will find acceptance.

o 88

* KI.MIN ana ⸢TI-šu₂⸣ u₂bu-uṭ-na-nu u₂ḫa-ša₂-nu u₂KUR.KUR

(o 8) Alternatively, in order to cure him: you dry (and) pound buṭnānu-plant, thyme, white hellebore, lord of the herbs, deer's phlegm, malt, (and) root of šunû-tree (and) have him drink it with wine on an empty stomach.

o 99

u₂EN U₂-MEŠ pi-⸢šer!⸣-ti a-a-li₃ šur*-ši ŠE!?.NU₂!?4

o 1010

ṣa-ṣa-ru BABBAR⸢u₂⸣ ḪAD₂.A SUD₂ ina GEŠTIN la₃ pa-tan NAG-[šu₂]

Reverse
r 1r 1

[EN₂] ⸢dNIN.MAḪ⸣ AMA rem-ni-tu₂

(r 1) Incantation: Ninmah, merciful mother;

r 22

⸢dNIN⸣.MAḪ ⸢re⸣-ma TUKUa

(r 2) Ninmah, have mercy on me;

r 33

dNIN.MAḪ ⸢suḫ⸣-ḫi-ir pa-ni-ki

(r 3) Ninmah, turn your face;

r 44

dNIN.MAḪ [nap]-šir a-a-ši TU₆ EN₂

(r 4) Ninmah, relent towards me. Incantation formula.

(rest of reverse blank)

1In view of such phrases as qāt Ninmah (l.1), ana bulluṭišu (l.8), and mag[ir]? (l.7) the excerpt is rather medical-exorcistic than namburbû. Parallel: ll. 1-4 // BAM 115, 14’-16’. ll. 1-4 // BAM 115, 14’-16’; its variants that make a difference are: (l.1) KÀŠ-šú, {d}be-let--DINGIR-MEŠ; (l.2) [x]-ha ŠUB{+di}; (l.3) seems to omit UDU.S[ISK]UR BAL{+qí} DUG.A.DA.GUR₅ GUB{+an}; (l.4) [NÍG.N]A ŠIM.LI GAR{+an}; (ll.3-4) ... GAR{+an} would seem to be followed by KAŠ GE[ŠTIN BAL GURUN KIRI₆ DÙ.A.BI GAR{+an} ...].

2A sequence ... tasarraq ... tašakkan is what one would expect.

3Alternatively to be restored as d[i-ni di]-ni, 'render verdict for me.'

4The drug epithet or name '(the) lord of the herbs' would appear to be unique.
